title: Psalms 68
subtitle: May God Arise, Let His Enemies Be Scattered

heb.paragraph: "The chariots (rek̅eb) of God are tens of thousands and thousands of thousands; the Lord has come from Sinai into his sanctuary" (v.17). The great processional psalm — the longest and most complex in the Psalter. God arises (v.1, echoing the ark-march of Num 10:35). He is a father to the fatherless, a defender of widows (v.5). He sets the lonely in families, leads out prisoners with singing (v.6). The Sinai theophany is recalled: earth shook, heavens poured rain (vv.7–8). God ascends the mountain with captives (v.18). Paul quotes v.18 in Ephesians 4:8 for Christ’s ascension.

notes.note: "May God arise, may his enemies be scattered." The ark-march cry (Num 10:35). God goes to war.

"A father to the fatherless, a defender of widows, is God in his holy dwelling. God sets the lonely in families, he leads forth the prisoners with singing." God’s first action: care for the vulnerable. Before the military triumph, the social justice.

"The chariots of God are tens of thousands. The Lord has come from Sinai into his sanctuary. When you ascended on high, you took many captives." Paul applies v.18 to Christ’s ascension (Eph 4:8): Christ ascended and gave gifts to humanity.

sections.header: Verses 19–35 — “Praise Be to the Lord, Who Daily Bears Our Burdens”

heb.paragraph: "Proclaim the power (ʿōz) of God, whose majesty is over Israel, whose power is in the heavens" (v.34). God daily bears burdens (v.19), provides escape from death (v.20), and marches in procession into the sanctuary (vv.24–27). The tribes of Israel gather (v.27). Kings bring tribute (v.29). God is praised in the great assembly (v.26). "Sing to God, you kingdoms of the earth" (v.32). The psalm closes: "he gives power and strength to his people" (v.35).

notes.note: "Praise be to the Lord, to God our Saviour, who daily bears our burdens." Not once but DAILY. Every day’s weight is carried by God.

"Your procession, God, has come into view... the singers before, the musicians after, with them the young women playing timbrels." The processional scene: music, movement, celebration. All twelve tribes represented.

"Proclaim the power of God, whose majesty is over Israel, whose power is in the heavens. You, God, are awesome in your sanctuary; the God of Israel gives power and strength to his people." The psalm’s climactic doxology: heavenly power channelled to earthly people.

ppl.name: God (Elohim / Yahweh / Adonai)
ppl.role: Warrior-processional God whose march from Sinai to Zion conquers all enemies (vv.1-10,17-18,24-35)
ppl.text: This is the Psalter's grandest processional hymn — a theological history compressed into 35 verses. God's march begins at Sinai (v.8, quoting Judg 5:4-5), moves through the wilderness (vv.7-10), defeats kings (vv.11-14), and arrives at Zion (vv.15-18). The 'chariots of God are tens of thousands and thousands of thousands' (v.17). Paul quotes verse 18 ('When you ascended on high, you took many captives; you received gifts from people') in Ephesians 4:8 as Christ's ascension, adapting 'received' to 'gave.' God is simultaneously 'a father to the fatherless' and a 'defender of widows' (v.5) — cosmic warrior and social protector.

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: Attributed author orchestrating a processional liturgy (superscription, v.24-27)
ppl.text: David describes the worship procession: 'Your procession, God, has come into view, the procession of my God and King into the sanctuary. In front are the singers, after them the musicians; with them are the young women playing timbrels' (vv.24-25). The tribal roll call — Benjamin, Judah, Zebulun, Naphtali (v.27) — represents all Israel. This processional order may reflect the actual liturgy when the ark entered Jerusalem (2 Sam 6).

ppl.name: The Defeated Kings
ppl.text: When God 'scattered the kings in the land' (v.14), the women 'divide the plunder' (v.12). The enigmatic image of a dove with 'wings sheathed with silver, its feathers with shining gold' (v.13) may represent Israel adorned with spoils of war. Mount Bashan 'looks with envy at the mountain where God chooses to reign' (v.16) — even nature's most imposing peaks are subordinate to God's chosen dwelling on modest Zion.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVMay you blow them away like smoke— as wax melts before the fire, may the wicked perish before God.ESVAs smoke is driven away, so you shall drive them away; as wax melts before fire, so the wicked shall perish before God!NIVMay God arise, may his enemies be scattered; may his foes flee before him.ESVGod shall arise, his enemies shall be scattered; and those who hate him shall flee before him!

rec.text: ""May God arise, may his enemies be scattered." The ark-march cry (Num 10:35). God goes to war."

rows.label: The Procession — 68:19–35
rows.text: "Who daily bears our burdens." Kings bring tribute. "Sing to God, you kingdoms." Power to his people.

hebtext.note: "The chariots (rek̅eb) of God are tens of thousands and thousands of thousands; the Lord has come from Sinai into his sanctuary" (v.17). The great processional psalm — the longest and most complex in the Psalter. God arises (v.1, echoing the ark-march of Num 10:35). He is a father to the fatherless, a defender of widows (v.5). Paul quotes v.18 in Ephesians 4:8 for Christ’s ascension.

God daily bears burdens (v.19), provides escape from death (v.20), and marches in procession into the sanctuary (vv.24–27). The tribes of Israel gather (v.27). Kings bring tribute (v.29). themes.note: Sovereignty peaks at 10: the great processional — tens of thousands of chariots, Sinai to sanctuary. Worship at 9. Prophecy at 8: Eph 4:8 ascension. Justice: father to the fatherless. Mission: "sing to God, you kingdoms."
